title: Cyclotron mass-selective de Haas-van Alphen measurements using temperature modulation
authors: Michelle Hollricher, Andreas Bauer, Leo Maximov, Louw Feenstra, Christian Pfleiderer, Marc A. Wilde
abstract: We present a temperature-modulated de Haas-van Alphen measurement technique that allows selective addressing of quantum oscillations with different effective masses $m^{\ast}$ using a non-monotonic amplitude evolution with temperature and magnetic field, governed by the temperature derivative of the Lifshitz-Kosevich factor. The technique relies on harmonic modulation of the sample temperature and phase-sensitive detection of quantum oscillations in the voltage induced in a pick-up coil. We use a set of frequencies with strong Zeeman-driven harmonic content in the compensated topological semimetal MoSi$_{2}$ as a natural linear mass comb ranging from 1$m^{\ast}$ to 13$m^{\ast}$ to demonstrate the tunability of the mass-dependent quantum oscillation amplitudes experimentally. The technique allows to reliably isolate weak contributions of heavy orbits that are inaccessible in conventional de Haas-van Alphen frequency spectra because their frequency peaks overlap with much stronger frequency peaks of lighter orbits.
summary: This work introduces and validates the temperature-modulated de Haas-van Alphen ($	ext{TM-dHvA}$) technique, which measures the temperature derivative of magnetization oscillations. This method overcomes the limitation of overlapping frequency peaks by exploiting the mass-dependent non-monotonic behavior of $\partial M / \partial T$. It successfully demonstrates the independent measurement of heavy and light cyclotron masses in MoSi2, opening a new avenue for studying complex Fermi surfaces.
main_problem: The primary challenge is selectively measuring quantum oscillations from orbits with widely different effective masses ($m^*$) when their frequency peaks overlap in conventional de Haas-van Alphen (dHvA) measurements.
main_result: The temperature-modulated dHvA ($	ext{TM-dHvA}$) technique successfully achieves cyclotron mass selectivity, allowing the independent extraction of heavy and light orbit contributions even when they are indistinguishable by frequency alone.
method: The method involves sinusoidally modulating the sample temperature ($T$) and measuring the phase-sensitive voltage signal proportional to $\partial M / \partial T$, leveraging the non-monotonic temperature dependence of the Lifshitz-Kosevich factor.
model_or_system: The experiment uses single-crystal compensated topological semimetal MoSi2 as a platform, which naturally exhibits a rich spectrum of quantum oscillations with multiple distinct cyclotron masses ($1 m^*$ to $13 m^*$).
key_observables: Demodulated voltage signal proportional to $\partial M / \partial T$, analyzed via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) across varying temperatures and magnetic fields.
important_parameters: Low temperatures ($T < 1	ext{ K}$) enhance the visibility of heavy orbits; the temperature modulation amplitude ($\Delta T$) is crucial for signal detection.
main_assumptions: The technique assumes that the measured oscillation component is proportional to $\partial M / \partial T$, which simplifies analysis by exploiting the temperature derivative of the Lifshitz-Kosevich factor.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how conventional dHvA struggles with overlapping frequencies, while TM-dHvA clearly separates contributions from heavy and light masses into distinct low-temperature and high-temperature regimes on the $\partial M / \partial T$ signal.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitations of standard dHvA measurements, details the temperature modulation technique ($	ext{TM-dHvA}$), presents simulations demonstrating mass separation in principle (using overlapping frequencies), and finally applies this method to experimental data on MoSi2 to extract multiple cyclotron masses.
why_interesting: While focused on solid-state physics, the ability to selectively probe different energy scales or effective parameters ($m^*$) via a derivative measurement technique is analogous to advanced spectroscopic techniques used in AMO physics for characterizing complex quantum states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Field and temperature dependence of the Lifshitz– Kosevich factor RT and its derivative ∂RT/∂T . (a) RT vs T at 10 T for selected m∗. In a conventional dHvA experiment, light m∗tend to dominate the dHvA spectrum at all T . Mod- ulation with amplitude ∆T results in oscillations of RT with amplitude ∆RT. The demodulated induced voltage U scales with ∂RT/∂T . (b) ∂RT/∂T vs T . Oscillations with light m∗are strongly suppressed at low T , where oscillations with heavy m∗dominate and vice versa. (c) RT vs B at 300 mK. In a conventional dHvA experiment, light m∗dominate the signal at all B. (d) ∂RT/∂T vs B. Oscillations with light m∗are strongly suppressed at high B, where oscillations...

FIG. 2. TM-dHvA setup. (a) Schematic side view show- ing the sample (S) attached to a heater (H) and thermome- ter (TH) platform, thermally linked to the bath (B). The sam- ple is situated inside the pick-up coil (PC). (b) Cutaway view of the setup showing the sample (S), mounted on a modiﬁed bare RuO2 chip that is used as both heater (H) and ther- mometer (TH), situated inside the pick-up coil (PC).

FIG. 3. Quantum oscillations, Fermi surface and extremal orbits of MoSi2 for B ∥c. The raw demodulated voltage U at T0 = 690 mK and f = 22.08 Hz is shown as a function of 1/B. The non-oscillatory background is negligible compared to the quantum oscillation amplitudes. The Fermi surface of MoSi2 exhibits one extremal electron orbit β, one minimal hole orbit α1 and two symmetry-equivalent maximal hole orbits α2.

FIG. 4. Field and temperature evolution of TM-dHvA oscillation amplitudes in MoSi2. (a) FFT spectrum of the data in Fig. 3(a) over a large window between 1/15 and 1/2 T−1. Frequencies α1, α2 and β and harmonic content up to p = 13 are indicated by vertical lines. The pth harmonics of α1 and α2 correspond to orbits with masses of p m∗ α1,2. (b) FFT spectrum for a narrow window of 1/30 T−1 width centered at 1/Bm = 1/4 T−1 and T0 = 690 mK. Here, signals from 1α1,2 dominate the spectrum. (c) As in (b) but with the window centered at 1/Bm = 1/12 T−1. Here, the orbits with large masses dominate, and the light fundamentals are suppressed. (d) As in (c) but at T0 = 4.05 K. Fig. 4(c), where signal from the harmonic content asso- ciated with heavy m∗dominates, the fundamental peaks are present at 4.05 K, while the amplitudes of higher har- monics are suppressed. It follows that the choice of ﬁeld window position and temperature range determines the range of orbit masses m∗that contribute to the FFT spectrum of the TM-dHvA oscillations. To analyze the oscillation amplitudes of individual har- monics, we ﬁrst computed FFTs using the large window from 1/15 to 1/2 T−1, isolated individual FFT peaks us- ing a rectangular window, and transformed the signal back via an inverse FFT (IFFT).
